coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical apta-biosensor for zeatin detection based on graphene quantum dots improved photoactivity of graphite-like carbon nitride and streptavidin induced signal inhibition
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yue
Zhou, Yunlei
Xu, Lv
Han, Zhenwei
Yin, Huanshun
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: Zeatin is a kind of typical cytokinin, which plays important roles in regulating the proliferation and differentiation of plant cells, and widely distributed in various plant tissues. Herein, a simple and sensitive photoelectrochemical method was developed for zeatin detection using graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as photoactive material, graphene quantum dots as photoactivity improvement reagent, gold nanoparticles as immobilization substrate for DNA probe, DNA biotin labeled aptamer as zeatin recognition and streptavidin capture element, and streptavidin as PEC response inhibitor factor. In the presence of zeatin, DNA aptamer can bind with zeatin to form conjugate, which make aptamer releasing from electrode surface and decrease the capture amount of streptavidin, causing the increase of the PEC response. Under optimal conditions, the biosensor showed wide linear range from 0.1 to 100nM and low detection limit of 0.031nM (3σ). The developed method also presented good selectivity, even discriminating zeatin analogues. In addition, the proposed detection strategy can be further applied to detect zeatin in complex biological matrix, revealing great potential in practical applicability. This work might provide an excellent platform for zeatin or other phytohormone detection using aptamer as recognition reagent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical biosensor
Graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheets
Gold nanoparticles
Aptamer
Biotin-streptavidin system

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of g-C3N4 nanosheets and AuNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the PEC biosensor fabrication and zeatin detection based on g-C3N4 and DNA aptamer.
TEM images of g-C3N4 (A) and AuNPs (B).

PEC response of different electrodes in 10mM PBS containing 0.1M AA, pH 7.4. For (A), (a) ITO, (b) g-C3N4/ITO, (c) GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, (d) AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, (e) ssDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, (f) dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, (g) dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO after Exo I incubation, (h) SA/dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO. For (B), (a) dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, (b) dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO after incubated with zeatin, (c) dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO after incubated with zeatin and Exo I, successively. (d) dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO after incubated with zeatin, Exo I and SA, successively. (e) SA/dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO. Zeatin concentration was 5nM.
The effect of g-C3N4 concentration (A), hybridization time (B), streptavidin immobilization time (C), zeatin and aptamer reaction time (D) on PEC response.

(A) PEC response of the biosensor with different concentrations of zeatin in 10mM PBS and 0.1M AA, pH 7.4. Curve a – g, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100nM. Inset graph, the linear relationship between the logarithm value of zeatin concentration and photocurrent. (B) The histogram for the photocurrent change of the biosensor fabricated with different phytohormones. (C) Time–based photocurrent responses of the biosensors with light on and off. (D) The histogram for PEC responses with nine biosensors fabricated with same process.
Comparison of detection performances of PEC method with other detection techniques.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a, detection target is zeatin riboside. MSPE-UPLC−MS/MS, magnetic solid-phase extraction coupled withultra performance liquid chromatography−tandem mass spectrometry. UHPLC Q-TOF MS, ultrahigh-pressure liquid chromatography coupled to high-definition accurate mass quadrupole time of flight mass spectrometry. CZE-MS/MS, capillary zone electrophoresis-tandem mass spectrometry. HPLC-QTOF MS, high performance liquid chromatography-quadrupole-time of flight mass spectrometry. mMI-SPELC–MS/MS, monolithic molecularly imprinted solid-phase extraction followed by liquid chromatography-electrospray tandem mass spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The recovery test of the developed method for zeatin detection in 10% plant tissue culture.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical apta-biosensor for zeatin detection based on graphene quantum dots improved photoactivity of graphite-like carbon nitride and streptavidin induced signal inhibition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, 271018, Taian, Shandong, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Institute of Engineering, 411104, Xiangtan, Hunan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zeatin is a kind of typical cytokinin, which plays important roles in regulating the proliferation and differentiation of plant cells, and widely distributed in various plant tissues. Herein, a simple and sensitive photoelectrochemical method was developed for zeatin detection using graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as photoactive material, graphene quantum dots as photoactivity improvement reagent, gold nanoparticles as immobilization substrate for DNA probe, DNA biotin labeled aptamer as zeatin recognition and streptavidin capture element, and streptavidin as PEC response inhibitor factor. In the presence of zeatin, DNA aptamer can bind with zeatin to form conjugate, which make aptamer releasing from electrode surface and decrease the capture amount of streptavidin, causing the increase of the PEC response. Under optimal conditions, the biosensor showed wide linear range from 0.1 to 100nM and low detection limit of 0.031nM (3σ). The developed method also presented good selectivity, even discriminating zeatin analogues. In addition, the proposed detection strategy can be further applied to detect zeatin in complex biological matrix, revealing great potential in practical applicability. This work might provide an excellent platform for zeatin or other phytohormone detection using aptamer as recognition reagent.

ce:section.ce:para: Cytokinins (CKs), a family of N6-substituted adenine derivatives, are a kind of crucially natural phytohormone, which can regulating the proliferation and differentiation of plant cells, and also control diverse processes in plant growth and development, including seed germination, de-etiolation, chloroplast differentiation, apical dominance, plantpathogen interactions, flower and fruit development, and leaf senescence [1,2]. Zeatin is the first identified naturally occurring cytokinin extracting from the grain filling period of sweet corn, which is the major active cytokinin and widely distributed in various plant tissues [3,4].

With the fast development of analytical techniques, more sensitive and accurate detection methods for zeatin have been achieved, including mass spectrometric technique sans chromatographic separation [5], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [6], high-performance liquid chromatography with electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry [7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8]. Though these methods have the advantages of high sensitivity and simultaneous detection of multiple phytohormones, they still require expensive instruments, skill operators, complicated pretreatment process, which limit their widely application for in situ assay.

Recently, photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor is a newly and promising determination technique based on the electron transfer among analyte, photoactive species and electrode under photo-irradiation. Due to the advantages of low background current, high sensitivity, fast response, low cost, and easy miniaturization of detection devices [9–11], PEC biosensor has been widely applied for detecting various analytes, such as DNA [12,13], microRNA [14,15], protein [16,17], small organic molecule [18,19], and metal ion [20–22]. For PEC analysis, a photoactive working electrode is required to provide photocurrent under photo-irradiation. Recently, graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted great attentions due to its high photocatalytic efficiency. As a kind of semiconductor material, g-C3N4 nanosheet also possesses photoelectric conversion performance and can produce photocurrent under visible light irradiation [23]. However, g-C3N4 nanosheets exist in the disadvantage of low photoelectric conversion efficiency. To improving this deficiency, composite g-C3N4 semiconducting materials with enhanced photo-activity, such as nitrogen-doped graphene/layered MoS2 [24], TiO2 [25], CdS quantum dots [16], reduced graphene oxide [26], have been adopted to substitute single semiconductor, which has been supposed to promising strategy for improving photoactivity of g-C3N4. These modifications probably could improve the detection sensitivity of PEC biosensor.

To achieve high detection specificity, specific recognition reagent for zeatin is also needed for PEC biosensor construction. Previously, antibody has been widely employed for specific recognition of zeatin [27]. However, the activity of antibody can be influenced greatly by the storage conditions and experimental conditions, and the antibody is also expensive. Aptamers are a kind of single-strand DNA or RNA with high affinity to its target. Compared with antibody, aptamers possess advantages of high affinity, low cost, easy synthesis and modification, remarkable target diversity and good stability [28,29]. On account of these benefits, aptamers have been widely applied in biosensor fabrication and various targets detection, such as metal ions [30,31], small molecule contaminants [32,33], proteins [34,35], bacteria [36,37] and viruses [38–40]. More importantly, zeatin aptamer has also been screened and two fluorescence methods based on aptamer for zeatin detection was developed [41,42]. However, no electrochemical-based method was investigated.

In this work, g-C3N4 nanosheets and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was explored for developing a PEC biosensing platform for the first time, where g-C3N4 nanosheets was served as highly efficient photoactive material, biotin-labeled zeatin-binding aptamer was employed as zeatin recognition element, and streptavidin was used as signal inhibition reagent. After composited with GQDs, the PEC response of g-C3N4 nanosheets improved greatly due to the excellent synergy effect between the PEC activities of g-C3N4 and GQDs, reducing the probability of the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole. In the presence of zeatin, the aptamer will bind with zeatin, and release from the double-strand DNA (dsDNA) on electrode surface, which make the failure of the formation of biotin-streptavidin conjugate on the electrode surface, and leads to a strong photocurrent. Based on it, a sensitive and selective PEC apta-biosensor was constructed for zeatin detection.

Melamine, ascorbic acid (AA), trans-zeatin sodium citrate, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), dithiothreitol (DTT), tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TECP), 6-benzylaminopurine, gibberellin, indole-3-acetic acid, indole-3-propionic acid, indole-3- butyric acid were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Tris-base was obtained from Solarbio (Shanghai, China). Streptavidin (SA), exonuclease I (Exo I) and DNA were purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China), and DNA sequences were listed as follows. Probe DNA, 5′-SH-CGG ATA AAC CTC TTA TGC CTG CCT AAC CAT ATC CG-3′, aptamer DNA, 5′-biotin-CGG ATA TGG TTA GGC AGG CAT AAG AGG TTT ATC CG-3′. Exo I storage buffer, 20mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 0.1mM EDTA, 1mM DTT and 50% (v/v) glycerol. Exo I reaction buffer, 67mM glycine-KOH (pH 9.5), 6.7mM MgCl2, 1mM DTT. DNA dissolution buffer (1×TE buffer), 10mM Tris–HCl, 1mM EDTA, pH 8.0. DNA immobilization buffer, 10mM Tris-HCl, 1.0mM EDTA, 1.0M NaCl, and 1.0mM TCEP, pH 7.0. DNA hybridization buffer, 100mM NaCl, 20mM Tris–HCl, 2mM MgCl2, 5mM KCl, pH 7.5. Detection buffer, 10mM PBS containing 0.1M AA, pH 7.4.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from JEM-1400PLUS (Japan).

2.0g melamine was loosely packed in a porcelain boat, and then heated in a Muffle furnace with heating rate of 20°C/min to 550°C for 4h [43]. The obtained yellow product was washed with ethanol and redistilled deionized water for three times, respectively. Then, the product was dried at 60°C for 6h. Afterwards, 2mg g-C3N4 was weighed accurately and dispersed in 4mL redistilled deionized water with the aid of ultrasonication for 30min. Subsequently, g-C3N4 suspension liquid was treated with probe sonicator (Scientz® JY92-IIDN, Zhejiang, China) for 30min with 300 cycles of 3s of sonication with an interval of 3s. Then, the suspension liquid was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 30min. The solid product was collected and washed with redistilled deionized water for three times. Finally, the product was dried at 60°C for 6h.

AuNPs were synthesized according to previous report [44]. In brief, 200mL two-neck flask, magnetic stir bar, glass stopper and spherical condenser in aqua regia for at least 15min. Then the glassware was rinsed with redistilled deionized water. After that, 98mL of redistilled deionized water and 2mL of 50mM HAuCl4 were added into the two-neck flask. Afterwards, the mixed solution was heated to reflux. Then, 10mL of 38.8mM was added into the flask, and the reaction system was allowed to reflux for another 20min. Subsequently, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature under stirring. Finally, the deep red solution was obtained.

The ITO electrode was first cleaned in acetone, ethanol/NaOH (1:1, volume ratio, NaOH concentration is 0.1M) and deionized water each for 30min ultrasonically. Then, 40μL of 2.0mg/mL g-C3N4 dispersion was dripped on ITO electrode surface, and dried under the irradiation of infrared lamp. The electrode was noted as g-C3N4/ITO. After that, g-C3N4/ITO electrode was further coated with 40μL of 0.25mg/mL GQDs and 40μL AuNPs dispersion successively. The obtained electrode was labeled as GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO and, respectively. Then, AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was incubated with 20μL of 1.0μM probe DNA for 120min at room temperature in a humid cell. The electrode was marked as ssDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO. The electrode was then sealed with 20μL of 0.1mM MPA for 60min. DNA hybridization was performed by incubating ssDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO with 20μL of 1μM aptamer DNA for 120min at 37°C under humid conditions. The electrode was named as dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO. Afterwards, the electrode was incubated with 20μL of Exo I buffer containing 100 unit/mL Exo I for 60min at 37°C, and followed with 20μL of 0.1mg/mL streptavidin (in 10mM Tris-HCl and 50mM NaCl) for 75min. The electrode was noted as SA/dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO.

For zeatin detection assay, 20μL of different concentrations of zeatin (in 10mM Tris-HCl and 50mM NaCl, pH 7.4) was dripped on dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO surface and incubated at room temperature for 120min. Then, the electrode was further incubated with Exo I and streptavidin successively.
After each modification process, the electrode was rinsed with washing buffer for 3 mins, and dried under N2 blowing.

PEC detections were performed at a homemade PEC system. A 500W Xe lamp was served as the irradiation source to generate the visible-light with the assistance of optical filter. The photocurrent was obtained at CHI832A electrochemical workstation (CHI instruments, Austin, USA) with a three-electrode system in PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4), which was deaerated by N2 for 0.5h before use. A modified ITO electrode with an area of 0.195cm2 was used as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode.

In this work, a simple analysis strategy was designed for zeatin detection with PEC technique. As seen in Scheme 1 , AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was first prepared, where g-C3N4 was used as photoactive material, GQDS was selected as photoactivity improvement material for g-C3N4, and AuNPs were employed as probe DNA immobilization matrix. Then, probe DNA with 5′-SH label was assembled on electrode surface based on the formation of Au-S bond between gold and −SH. Then, 5′-biotin labeled DNA aptamer was further modified on the electrode surface through hybridization to form double-strand DNA structure, which can effective block the digestion of Exo I towards single-strand DNA probe. However, through the specific interaction between biotin and streptavidin, streptavidin can be captured on the electrode surface. At this state, only a weak photocurrent can be obtained because the bulky protein of streptavidin can hinder the transfer of electron donor to electrode surface and increase the probability of the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole of photoactive material. Nonetheless, when dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was first incubated with zeatin, the target of aptamer, then with Exo I and straptavidin, a strong photocurrent can be achieved. In the presence of zeatin, DNA aptamer in dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO can bind with its target of zeatin to form a stable complex, which can lead to the release of biotin-labeled aptamer from electrode surface, and only single-strand DNA probe is remained. After digested by Exo I, the single-strand DNA probe can be further removed from the electrode surface. Due to the absence of biotin, streptavidin can not be captured, which improve the possibility of the diffusion of electron donor to electrode surface, and the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole can be inhibited. As a result, the photocurrent can be increased. In addition, the hydrolysis of probe DNA can further decrease the migration resistance of electron donor and enhance the PEC response. Therefore, according to the relationship between zeatin concentration and photocurrent, zeatin can be detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of g-C3N4 nanosheets and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of g-C3N4 and AuNPs were characterized by TEM. As presented in Fig. 1 A, g-C3N4 showed crumpled layered structure with stacking layers. Fig. 1B illustrated the TEM image of AuNPs, which showed mainly spherical structure, where the diameter was mainly lowered than 20nm. In addition, the morphology of the electrode g-C3N4/ITO and GODs/g-C3N4/ITO was characterized by SEM and the results were listed in Supplementary material (Fig. S1). g-C3N4 was also characterized by AFM (Fig. S2).

To characterize the biosensor fabrication process, the photocurrent of the biosensor was recorded with each step for fabrication in 10mM Tris-HCl containing 0.1M AA, pH 7.4. The photoelectrochemical response was recorded with light on at 60s and light off at 80s. As shown in Fig. 2 A, no photocurrent was observed at the bare ITO electrode (curve a), indicating that no photoactivity of ITO electrode and no photoactive material was modified on ITO electrode surface. However, an obvious photocurrent was obtained at g-C3N4/ITO (curve b), demonstrating the photoactivity of g-C3N4 nanosheets. Under visible light illumination, the photogenerated electrons can be captured by electrode to produce photocurrent, and some of the photogenerated holes can be captured by the donated electron from electron donor. However, due to the recombination of some photogenerated electrons and holes, the obtained photocurrent of g-C3N4/ITO was weak, which might influence the detection sensitivity. When GQDs were coated on g-C3N4/ITO surface, the photocurrent increased greatly (curve c), which might be attributed to the synergistic effect between GQDs and g-C3N4, improving the transfer of photogenerated electron to ITO electrode. After AuNPs were immobilized on GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO surface, the photocurrent decreased inversely (curve d). In the preparation process of AuNPs, citric acid was used as reduction reagent, which lead to that more citric acid can be adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs. Therefore, AuNPs were negative, which can repel the transfer of electron donor of AA towards electrode surface, resulting in the decreased photocurrent. Subsequently, the photocurrent decreased successively when probe DNA (curve e) and DNA aptamer (curve f) were immobilized on electrode surface, which can be ascribed to that the negatively charged DNA molecule blocked the diffusion of the negatively charged electron donor. After dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was incubated with Exo I, the photocurrent only increased little (curveg), indicating that only little probe DNA can be hydrolyzed because most probe DNA have hybridized with DNA aptamer, which protects probe DNA from hydrolysis of Exo I. Afterwards, the photocurrent decreased greatly when streptavidin was modfied on electrdoe surface based on the specific interaction between biotin and streptavidin (curveh). It can be attributed to the bulky protein, blocking the diffusion of electron donor of AA. According to the photocurrent change, the successful preparation process as described in Scheme 1a can be confirmed.

To further verify the feasibility for zeatin detection using the developed method, the PEC behaviors of ssDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO with different treatment process were investigated in 10mM PBS containing 0.1M AA, pH 7.4. As presented in Fig. 2B, curve a was the PEC response of dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO. After dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was incubated with 5nM zeatin, the obtained electrode presented increased photocurrent (curve b) when compared with dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, indicating that the formation of aptamer-zeatin conjugate could release DNA aptamer from the double helix structure and reduce the steric hindrance. The photocurrent further decreased when the electrode was further incubated with Exo I (curve c). Exo I could digest the single-strand probe DNA on electrode surface and further reduced baffle effect, which could increase the transfer rate of electron donor and reach a strong PEC response. However, when the electrode was then incubated with streptavidin, the photocurrent decreased (curve d), revealing that there were still some dsDNA (especially DNA aptamer) remained on electrode surface to capture streptavidin due to the insufficient concentration of zeatin. In addition, the photocurrent for curve d was greatly higher than that at SA/dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO (curve e), which further demonstrated the formation of aptamer-zeatin conjugate and the digestion of probe DNA by Exo I. Based on the above experiments, it can be concluded that the developed method can be used to detect zeation.

To achieve high detection sensitivity, several experimental conditions were optimized, such as g-C3N4 concentration, hybridization time, streptavidin immobilization time, zeatin and aptamer reaction time.

As photoactive material, g-C3N4 concentration can greatly influence the PEC response. To investigate its effect, different concentrations of g-C3N4 was modified on ITO electrode surface. As shown in Fig. 3 A, with increasing g-C3N4 concentration from 0.1 to 2mg/mL, the photocurrent enhanced gradually. Nevertheless, the photocurrent decreased when g-C3N4 concentration was higher than 2mg/mL. The reason for this decrease could be ascribed to the excessive g-C3N4 on the electrode surface to form a thickness, which decreased the transfer rate of the photogenerated electron to electrode and increased the possibility of the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole. When g-C3N4 concentration was higher than 5mg/mL, the photocurrent tended to level off, which might be ascribed to the fact that the thickness of g-C3N4 is thick enough and the photocurrent can be influenced little with further increasing g-C3N4 concentration.Therefore, 2mg/mL g-C3N4 was used in this work.

DNA aptamer was modified on electrode through its hybridization with probe DNA, therefore, the hybridization time was optimized. In this section, SA/dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was used as working electrode, which was fabricated with different hybridization time. As shown in Fig. 3B, the photocurrent decreased with extending hybridization time from 0 to 120min. Then a plateau was achieved when further prolonging the hybridization time. Thus 120min was chosen.

As photocurrent inhibition reagent, the immobilization time of streptavidin can greatly influence the PEC response. Thus streptavidin immobilization time was also optimized. As presented in Fig. 3C, the photocurrent decreased with increasing streptavidin immobilization time from 0 to 75min. And the photocurrent was achieved to minimum value with immobilization time of 75min. Thus this immobilization time was used as optimal condition.

The effect of zeatin and aptamer reaction time was also investigated. To perform it, dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO was first incubated with 5nM zeatin for different time, and then incubated with Exo I and streptavidin successively. The PEC response of the biosensor was illustrated in Fig. 3D. The photocurrent increased with extending reaction time from 0 to 120min, and then the photocurrent tended to level off with further increasing reaction time. Therefore, 120min was used in this work.

Under optimal experimental conditions, the relationship between zeatin concentration and photocurrent was investigated. The results were shown in Fig. 4 A. With increasing zeatin concentration, the photocurrent enhanced gradually. In the range of 0.1–100nM, a linear relationship could be constructed with the logarithm value of zeatin concentration and the photocurrent. The linear regression equation could be expressed as I =66.58logc +196.63 (R =0.9918, inset of Fig. 4A) and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.031nM (3σ). The detection performance of this work was compared with other methods and listed in Table 1 . The results indicated that the developed method possessed wide linear range and competitive detection limit.

The specificity of this method was evaluated using other analogues as possible interferents, such as, 6-benzylaminopurine (6-BAP), gibberellin (GB), indole-3-acetic acid (3-IAA), indole-3-propionic acid (3-IPA), indole-3- butyric acid (3-IBA).The photocurrent change (ΔI = I 2–I 1) was compared, where I 1 was the photocurrent of SA/dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO, and I 2 was the photocurrent of dsDNA/AuNPs/GQDs/g-C3N4/ITO after incubated with zeatin, Exo I and streptavidin, successively. As shown in Fig. 4B, the ΔI for zeatin was much higher than that for other analogues, indicating that the developed method had good detection selectivity. The stability of the photocurrent responses of the fabricated biosensor was also studied (Fig. 4C). No significant change of photocurrent response was observed with light on and off for 15 times repetition. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.51%, indicating a good stability. The reproducibility was assessed by detecting 1.0nM zeatin using nine biosensors, which were prepared independently with the same process. The RSD for these seven detections was 4.06% (Fig. 4D), implying satisfactory detection reproducibility.

To testify the potential applicability of the developed method in complex biological matrix, the recovery experiments were performed in 10% plant tissue culture after introducing different concentrations of zeatin. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of zeatin was in the range of 94% to 105%, which confirmed that the developed method could be applied to detect zeatin in complex biological matrix.

In summary, a simple and sensitive PEC strategy was developed for zeatin detection based on GQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite system, biotin-streptavidin system and Exonuclease I. The binding interaction between zeation and its DNA aptamer induced the release of aptamer from its double helix structure and kept aptamer away from electrode surface. It led to the reduction of the immobilized amount of streptavidin, and the increase of the photocurrent could be then achieved. Under the optimal experiment conditions, the developed method presented a good linear range for zeatin detection within 0.1–100nM, and the low detection limit of 0.031nM was achieved. In addition, the developed method also showed good selectivity, and zeatin in complex biological matrix could be detected. This work provided a general platform for other phytohormone detection using aptamer.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong province, China (No. ZR2016BM10, ZR2014BQ029), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21375079).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi: 10.1016/j.snb.2017.10.157.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Table S1
xxx.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cytokinins New insights into a classic phytohormone
Zeatin, a factor inducing cell division isolated from Zea mays
Zeatin modulates flower bud development and tocopherol levels in Cistus albidus (L.) plants as they age
Detection and quantification of some plant growth regulators in a seaweed-based foliar spray employing a mass spectrometric technique sans chromatographic separation
Phytohormone profiling of the sweet orange (Citrus sinensis (L.) Osbeck) leaves and roots using GC–MS-based method
Simultaneous analysis of ten phytohormones in Sargassum horneri by high-performance liquid chromatography with electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry
Comprehensive profiling of phytohormones in honey by sequential liquid–liquid extraction coupled with liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry
Photoelectrochemical bioanalysis: the state of the art
A new strategy for methylated DNA detection based on photoelectrochemical immunosensor using Bi2S3 nanorods, methyl bonding domain protein and anti-his tag antibody
CdS/MoS2 heterojunction-based photoelectrochemical DNA biosensor via enhanced chemiluminescence excitation
Photoelectrochemical immunosensor for microRNA detection based on gold nanoparticles-functionalized g-C3N4 and anti-DNA:RNA antibody
Signal-on photoelectrochemical biosensor for microRNA detection based on Bi2S3 nanorods and enzymatic amplification
Photoelectrochemical immunosensing platform for M. SssI methyltransferase activity analysis and inhibitor screening based on g-C3N4 and CdS quantum dots
An ultrasensitive energy-transfer based photoelectrochemical protein biosensor
Recognition unit-free and self-cleaning photoelectrochemical sensing platform on TiO2 nanotube photonic crystals for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine release from mouse brain
Photoelectrochemical immunosensing of tetrabromobisphenol A based on the enhanced effect of dodecahedral gold nanocrystals/MoS2 nanosheets
Photoelectrochemical sensing of Cu2+ ions with SnO2/CdS heterostructural films
Folding-based photoelectrochemical biosensor: binding-induced conformation change of a quantum dot-tagged DNA probe for mercury (II) detection
One-pot hydrothermal route to fabricate nitrogen doped graphene/Ag-TiO2: Efficient charge separation and high-performance on-off-on switch system based photoelectrochemical biosensing
Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based photocatalysts for artificial photosynthesis and environmental remediation: are we a step closer to achieving sustainability?
Constructing 2D porous graphitic C3N4 nanosheets/nitrogen-doped graphene/layered MoS2 ternary nanojunction with enhanced photoelectrochemical activity
Facile fabrication of an aptasensor for thrombin based on graphitic carbon nitride/TiO2 with high visible-light photoelectrochemical activity
Dual-responsive competitive immunosensor for sensitive detection of tumor marker on g-CN/rGO conjugation
Dynamics of cytokinins in apical shoot meristems of a day-neutral tobacco during floral transition and flower formation
Rapid and label-free strategy to isolate aptamers for metal ions
A mini-review on functional nucleic acids-based heavy metal ion detection
Ultrasensitive aptamer biosensor for malathion detection based on cationic polymer and gold nanoparticles
Application of DNA aptamers as sensing layers for detection of carbofuran by electrogenerated chemiluminescence energy transfer
Visual multiple recognition of protein biomarkers based on an array of aptamer modified gold nanoparticles in biocomputing to strip biosensor logic operations
On-chip, aptamer-based sandwich assay for detection of glycated hemoglobins via magnetic beads
Immediate detection of living bacteria at ultralow concentrations using a carbon nanotube based potentiometric aptasensor
Simultaneous detection of pathogenic bacteria using an aptamer based biosensor and dual fluorescence resonance energy transfer from quantum dots to carbon nanoparticles
Aptamer-based biosensors for the rapid visual detection of flu viruses
Ultrasensitive detection of influenza viruses with a glycan-based impedimetric biosensor
A fluorescent aptasensor for H5N1 influenza virus detection based-on the core-hhell nanoparticles metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF)
Target-induced structure switching of a hairpin aptamer for the fluorescence detection of zeatin
Simultaneous determination of zeatin and systemin by coupling graphene oxide-protected aptamers with catalytic recycling of DNase I, Sens. Actuators
Photodegradation performance of g-C3N4 fabricated by directly heating melamine
Preparation of aptamer-linked gold nanoparticle purple aggregates for colorimetric sensing of analytes
Efficient and selective enrichment of ultratrace cytokinins in plant samples by magnetic perhydroxy-cucurbit[8] uril microspheres
Assessing temporal flux of plant hormones in stored processing potatoes using high definition accurate mass spectrometry
High-internal-phase-emulsion polymeric monolith coupled with liquid chromatography-electrospray tandem mass spectrometry for enrichment and sensitive detection of trace cytokinins in plant samples

Simultaneous analysis of apolar phytohormones and 1-aminocyclopropan-1-carboxylic acid by high performance liquid chromatography/electrospray negative ion tandem mass spectrometry via 9-fluorenylmethoxycarbonyl chloride derivatization
Analysis of cytokinin nucleotides in coconut (Cocos nucifera L.) water using capillary zone electrophoresis-tandem mass spectrometry after solid-phase extraction

Analysis of phytohormones in vermicompost using a novel combinative sample preparation strategy of ultrasound-assisted extraction and solid-phase extraction coupled with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Sensitive quantification of isoprenoid cytokinins in plants by selective immunoaffinity purification and high performance liquid chromatography-quadrupole-time of flight mass spectrometry
Monolithic molecularly imprinted solid-phase extraction for the selective determination of trace cytokinins in plant samples with liquid chromatography-electrospray tandem mass spectrometry

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yue Wang is postgraduate student of College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. His current interests are photoelectrochemical analysis.

Yunlei Zhou received doctor degree in College of Life Science, Beijing Normal University in 2013. Now she is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. Her current interest is electrochemical biosensor.

Lv Xu received her master degree in College of Chemistry amd Chemical Engineering, Xiangtan University. Now, shi is lecture of School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Institute of Engineering. Her current interest is electrochemical biosensor.
Zhenwei Han is postgraduate student of College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. His current interest is preparation of functional nanomaterials.

Huanshun Yin received doctor degree in Soil Science in College of Resources and Environment, Shandong Agricultural University in 2012. Now he is an associate Professor in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. His current interests are bioelectroanalysis.

Shiyun Ai is a professor in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. He received his doctor degree (analytical chemistry) from Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University in 2004. His current research interests include bioelectroanalysis and preparation of nano-functional material.
